titleStmt.title: Letter from John Bradley to Sir Robert Chalmers, 20 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Bradley

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from John Bradley to Sir Robert Chalmers regarding broken wall between the Mountjoy Prison and North Circular Road Laundry.
Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938) was appointed under-secretary of Ireland in 1916, replacing Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) in the aftermath of the Easter Rising.

correspAction.placeName: The Treasury, Whitehall, London, S.W., England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Any reply to this letter should be addressed to—
THE SECRETARY,
TREASURY,
WHITEHALL, LONDON, S.W..
and the following number quoted.
6061
16 TREASURY CHAMBERS. 20 May 1916. Sir, I have laid before the Lords Commissioners of
His Majesty's Treasury Sir M. Nathan's letter of the 3rd
March last 3719/16 stating that during a storm in Dublin
on the night of the 12th November last a portion of the
wall, 15 feet high, forming the boundary between the
grounds attached to the Medical Officer's quarters at
Mountjoy Prison and those of the North Circular Laundry
Company was entirely blown down for length of 24 yards. In reply I am to enquire whether the needs of
the case could not be satisfactorily met at a far less
cost by the erection of a substantial wooden fence by
the Laundry Company subject to a contribution of one half
the cost by the Prisons Board. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, John Bradley The Under Secretary,
Dublin Castle. Send copy of this letter to
Prisons Board for their
observations.
I.J.T.
23.V.16
